findings.title: node_modules/ committed to git despite .gitignore
findings.description: 6453 files from node_modules/ are tracked in git. Initial commit was made before .gitignore existed.
findings.exploit_scenario: Bloats repo, exposes internal dependency code, could contain cached credentials or metadata.
findings.recommendation: Run: git rm -r --cached node_modules/ && git commit -m 'chore: remove tracked node_modules'

findings.title: dist/ build artifacts committed to git
findings.description: Build artifacts tracked in git. CI/CD builds fresh from source.
findings.exploit_scenario: Stale artifacts could contain outdated code with different security properties than source.
findings.recommendation: Run: git rm -r --cached dist/ && git commit -m 'chore: remove tracked dist'

findings.title: Unpinned GitHub Actions in deploy workflow
findings.description: All 5 actions use tag references (v4, v1, v3) instead of SHA pins. Third-party oven-sh/setup-bun@v1 is highest risk.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker compromises upstream action repo, force-pushes malicious code to the v1 tag. Next deploy runs the compromised action with write access to GitHub Pages.
findings.impact: Supply chain attack on deploy pipeline
findings.recommendation: Pin all actions to full commit SHAs.

findings.title: .gitignore missing .env patterns
findings.description: No .env, .env.*, or .env.local patterns in .gitignore. Accidental commit of environment files possible.
findings.exploit_scenario: Developer creates .env with API keys, runs git add -A, keys end up in public repo.
